---
title: "Structural Equation Modeling Overview"
format: pptx
---

## Structural Equation Modeling (SEM)

- SEM is a multivariate framework that combines factor analysis and multiple regression to model complex relationships among observed and latent variables.

## Key Concepts

- • Latent variables: unobserved constructs inferred from indicators.
- • Observed variables: directly measured items.
- • Measurement model: specifies loadings of indicators on latent constructs.
- • Structural model: specifies causal paths among latent constructs.
- • Model identification & fit assessment.

## Estimation Methods

- • Maximum Likelihood (ML): assumes multivariate normality.
- • Generalized Least Squares (GLS).
- • Weighted Least Squares (WLS) & Diagonal WLS (DWLS) for ordinal data.
- • Robust ML / Satorra–Bentler for non‑normality.

## Fit Indices

- • Chi‑square (χ²), RMSEA, SRMR, CFI, TLI, NFI, GFI, PNFI, PGFI.
- • Typical cut‑offs: RMSEA <0.05 good, CFI >0.95 good, SRMR <0.08 good.

## Applications

- • Psychology & Education: latent constructs, mediation.
- • Health Sciences: disease pathways.
- • Marketing: brand equity models.
- • Social Sciences: policy evaluation, invariance.
- • Economics: simultaneous equations.

## Practical Tips

- 1. Start with a clear theory.
- 2. Check identification.
- 3. Choose estimator based on data.
- 4. Report multiple fit indices.
- 5. Avoid cherry‑picking.
- 6. Use robust software (Mplus, lavaan, AMOS).
